title: Shodan keys should not be disclosed
description: Shodan is a search engine for internet-connected devices, infrastructure, and services, often called 'the search engine for the Internet of Things'. Unlike traditional search engines that index websites, Shodan continuously scans the entire IPv4 address space probing common ports and services to catalog exposed devices including servers, IoT devices, industrial control systems (ICS/SCADA), webcams, routers, smart home devices, databases, and network infrastructure. Shodan API keys are 32-character hexadecimal authentication credentials that provide programmatic access to Shodan's massive device database containing over 500 million indexed services. These keys grant access to: device search across 200+ countries filtering by port, protocol, service, banner, vulnerability, organization, or geographic location, banner information revealing software versions and service configurations (Apache, nginx, SSH, FTP, Telnet, databases, etc.), vulnerability intelligence showing devices affected by specific CVEs (Heartbleed, EternalBlue, Log4Shell, etc.), exploit search identifying devices vulnerable to known exploits, network intelligence revealing organizational infrastructure and IP ranges, honeypot detection and security research data, historical data showing device availability over time, DNS records and subdomain enumeration, SSL/TLS certificate information and expired certificates, industrial control system discovery (Modbus, S7, BACnet, HART-IP protocols), and bulk data exports for security research and threat intelligence. Exposing Shodan API keys enables attackers to: conduct reconnaissance on your organization's infrastructure identifying exposed services, open ports, and vulnerable devices, discover internet-facing assets that should be internal (databases, admin panels, IoT devices, ICS/SCADA systems), identify zero-day vulnerable devices before patches are applied, map your network topology and connected systems, discover shadow IT and unauthorized cloud deployments, access detailed banner information revealing software versions for exploit development, enumerate subdomains and DNS records for phishing campaigns, identify weak or default credentials on exposed services, discover misconfigured cloud storage (S3 buckets, Azure blobs) and databases, locate exposed webcams and surveillance systems revealing physical security, identify critical infrastructure vulnerabilities (power grids, water treatment, manufacturing), exhaust API query quotas (100-10,000 queries/month depending on plan) blocking legitimate security research, steal competitive intelligence by analyzing competitor infrastructure, access historical scan data revealing infrastructure changes over time, and conduct large-scale automated attacks targeting specific vulnerable device types.
recommendation: Never hardcode Shodan API keys in source code, configuration files, or version control. Immediately revoke the exposed API key from Shodan account dashboard (Account → API Key → Generate New Key), as Shodan does not support multiple keys per account, generating a new key automatically revokes the old one. Audit recent API query history by checking your account dashboard for: unusual search patterns, queries for your organization's IPs revealing reconnaissance attempts, queries for specific vulnerabilities, bulk data downloads, and API calls from unexpected geographic locations or IP addresses. Store API keys securely using environment variables (SHODAN_API_KEY), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration. Implement least privilege by using separate Shodan accounts for different teams or research projects. Use free tier accounts (limited queries) for development and testing to minimize exposure impact. Rotate API keys regularly (every 90 days recommended) by generating new keys and updating applications. Monitor API usage patterns through Shodan dashboard for anomalies: sudden query spikes, searches targeting your own infrastructure, vulnerability-specific queries (Heartblue, EternalBlue, Log4Shell), ICS/SCADA device searches, or geographic anomalies. Set up application-level rate limiting to prevent quota exhaustion from compromised keys. Implement IP whitelisting if your research infrastructure uses static addresses. Store query results securely as they may contain sensitive reconnaissance data. Never commit Shodan query results, exported data, or scan reports to version control. Use Shodan's streaming API cautiously as it provides real-time device discovery. Implement access controls on internal security research tools using Shodan API. Review organizational policies on offensive security research and reconnaissance activities.
impact: Critical security risk - Exposed Shodan API keys grant unauthorized access to internet-scale device discovery and vulnerability intelligence enabling attackers to: conduct reconnaissance on organizational infrastructure identifying all internet-facing assets, exposed services, and vulnerable devices (servers, IoT, ICS/SCADA, webcams, routers, databases), discover zero-day vulnerable systems before patches are applied, map complete network topology revealing organizational structure and cloud deployments, identify shadow IT and unauthorized cloud services, access detailed software version information for targeted exploit development, enumerate DNS records and subdomains for phishing campaigns and lateral movement, discover misconfigured cloud storage and exposed databases containing sensitive data, locate exposed webcams revealing physical security layouts, identify critical infrastructure vulnerabilities in power grids, water treatment, or manufacturing systems, exhaust API query quotas disrupting legitimate security operations, steal competitive intelligence by analyzing competitor infrastructure and technology stack, access historical scan data revealing infrastructure evolution and incident response patterns, and conduct automated bulk scanning for specific vulnerability patterns across millions of devices. For security teams, compromised keys expose ongoing research projects, reveal organizational security posture, and enable adversaries to identify and exploit vulnerabilities before defensive measures are implemented.

compliant.description: Using environment variables for Shodan credentials
compliant.code:
import os
import shodan

# Load Shodan key from environment
SHODAN_API_KEY = os.environ.get('SHODAN_API_KEY')

api = shodan.Shodan(SHODAN_API_KEY)

checks.message: Shodan API key detected in source code. This key provides access to Shodan's internet-scale device search engine containing over 500 million indexed services across 200+ countries. Exposing this key allows attackers to: conduct reconnaissance on your organization's infrastructure identifying all internet-facing assets, exposed services, open ports, vulnerable devices (servers, IoT, ICS/SCADA systems, webcams, routers, databases, industrial control systems), and misconfigured cloud services, discover devices affected by specific vulnerabilities (CVE searches for Heartbleed, EternalBlue, Log4Shell, ProxyLogon, etc.) before your security team patches them enabling targeted exploitation, map your complete network topology revealing organizational structure, subsidiaries, IP ranges, ASN ownership, cloud deployments, and data center locations, identify shadow IT and unauthorized cloud services (exposed S3 buckets, Azure blobs, databases, admin panels, APIs) that bypass security controls, access detailed service banner information showing exact software versions, configurations, default credentials, SSL/TLS certificate details, and service-specific metadata enabling precise exploit selection, enumerate your DNS records, subdomains, mail servers, and domain infrastructure for phishing campaigns, business email compromise (BEC), and lateral movement attacks, discover misconfigured cloud storage containing sensitive data (customer records, credentials, source code, financial data, intellectual property), locate internet-exposed webcams and surveillance systems revealing physical security layouts, employee movements, manufacturing processes, or data center access points, identify critical infrastructure vulnerabilities in power grids, water treatment facilities, manufacturing SCADA systems, building management systems, and transportation infrastructure, exhaust your API query quota (100 queries/month free tier, 10,000/month paid plans at $59-$899/month) blocking legitimate security research, vulnerability assessment, and threat intelligence operations, steal competitive intelligence by analyzing competitor technology stacks, infrastructure scale, geographic presence, service providers, security posture, and technical capabilities, access historical scan data revealing infrastructure changes over time showing incident response patterns, patch management effectiveness, infrastructure expansions, and security improvements, conduct automated bulk scanning for specific vulnerability patterns across millions of devices enabling large-scale exploitation campaigns, identify honeypots and security research infrastructure revealing your defensive capabilities and detection mechanisms, discover expired SSL/TLS certificates enabling man-in-the-middle attacks, locate exposed industrial protocols (Modbus TCP, S7, BACnet, HART-IP, DNP3, OPC UA) in critical infrastructure, search for default credentials on exposed services (admin/admin, root/root, etc.), identify misconfigured databases (MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Redis, CouchDB, Cassandra) with no authentication, and perform bulk data exports for offline analysis and attack planning. IMMEDIATELY revoke this API key from Shodan account dashboard at https://account.shodan.io (Note: Shodan allows only one API key per account, generating a new key automatically revokes the old one), generate replacement key and update applications, audit recent API usage in account dashboard checking for: unusual query patterns (port:22, port:3389, port:445), searches targeting your organization's IP ranges or domains, vulnerability-specific searches (product:"Apache 2.4.49" CVE-2021-41773), ICS/SCADA device queries (Modbus, S7, BACnet), bulk data downloads, streaming API usage, queries from unexpected geographic locations or IP addresses, and searches revealing reconnaissance activities (org:"YourCompany" or net:"YourIPRange"). Review query history for sensitive searches that may indicate attacker reconnaissance. Store API keys securely using environment variables (SHODAN_API_KEY) never hardcoded in source code, secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager at aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id shodan/api-key, HashiCorp Vault at vault kv get secret/shodan/key, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration files (.env files never committed to git, Docker secrets, Kubernetes secrets). Implement least privilege by using separate Shodan accounts for: different research teams, development vs production environments, specific security projects, and external consultants or partners. Use free tier accounts (100 queries/month) for development and testing to limit exposure impact if compromised. Rotate API keys every 90 days by generating new keys in dashboard and updating all applications, scripts, and automation workflows. Monitor API usage patterns and set alerts for: sudden query spikes (>50% baseline), searches targeting your own IP ranges (defense evasion reconnaissance), vulnerability-specific searches (CVE, exploit, 0day keywords), ICS/SCADA device searches (critical infrastructure targeting), geographic anomalies (queries from unexpected countries), off-hours activity (queries at 2-5 AM), streaming API activation (real-time device discovery), and bulk data exports (massive intelligence gathering). Implement application-level rate limiting: free tier max 1 query/second, paid accounts max 10 queries/second, daily query caps (100/day dev, 1000/day prod), and request throttling to prevent quota exhaustion from compromised keys. Use IP whitelisting for research infrastructure if static IPs available. Store query results securely as they contain reconnaissance data (exposed devices, vulnerabilities, IP ranges). Never commit Shodan data exports, scan results, or vulnerability reports to version control. Configure .gitignore: shodan_results.json, *.shodan, scan_data/. Implement access controls on security research tools using Shodan API: role-based access control (RBAC), audit logging for all queries, approval workflows for sensitive searches (ICS/SCADA, critical infrastructure), and segregation of research environments. Review organizational policies on offensive security research: authorized reconnaissance targets only, ethical disclosure for discovered vulnerabilities, compliance with computer fraud laws (CFAA, Computer Misuse Act), and responsible use of intelligence data. Use Shodan's streaming API cautiously as it provides real-time global device discovery with massive data volume. Document all Shodan searches for compliance and audit purposes. Train teams on proper use: search syntax (port:, country:, org:, net:), API capabilities, legal restrictions, and ethical considerations.
